message:
feat(terminal): add focus lock modes for automatic brain tracking, layer name display, and improved help visibility

This commit introduces intelligent focus lock modes enabling automatic brain tracking of top predators and oldest agents, adds visual layer name display for multi-layer networks, and improves help panel readability with a dimmed backdrop overlay.

FOCUS LOCK MODES: Automatic agent tracking for brain visualization

New FocusLockMode enum with three modes:
- Manual: User controls focus with ←→ arrow keys (default, previous behavior)
- TopPredator: Automatically focuses brain of current highest-scoring carnivore
- Oldest: Automatically focuses brain of currently oldest living agent

Mode switching via keyboard (scriptbots-app/src/terminal/mod.rs):
- `m` or `M` key: switch to Manual mode, restores direct arrow key control
- `t` or `T` key: switch to TopPredator mode, locks to leaderboard top entry
- `o` or `O` key: switch to Oldest mode, locks to age leaderboard top entry
- Each mode switch pushes event to event log for confirmation ("Focus: Manual", "Focus: Top predators", "Focus: Oldest agents")
- refresh_snapshot() called immediately after mode change to update brain display

Why focus lock modes matter:
- **Research workflows**: Studying evolutionary strategies requires observing successful agents over time
- **Performance analysis**: Top predators reveal which brain patterns dominate in current environment
- **Longevity studies**: Oldest agents show survival strategies; their brains adapt over hundreds of ticks
- **Hands-free monitoring**: Previously required constant arrow key presses to track agents as population changes
- **Dynamic populations**: Agent IDs change constantly (births/deaths); manual tracking breaks when focused agent dies

TopPredator mode implementation:
- Queries snap.leaderboard.first() to get highest kill count entry
- Leaderboard entry contains label field (agent FFI representation)
- Searches world.agents().iter_handles() to find handle matching label
- Returns Some(agent_id) if found, None if leaderboard empty or agent died
- Automatically updates each frame as leaderboard changes

Oldest mode implementation:
- Queries snap.oldest.first() to get longest-lived agent entry
- Similar label-based lookup through agent handles
- Tracks agent with maximum age counter
- Handles edge case: when oldest agent dies, immediately switches to new oldest

Manual mode implementation:
- Preserves original behavior: uses focused_agent_cursor % agent_count to index into population
- Arrow keys increment/decrement focused_agent_cursor
- Wraps cursor when reaching population boundaries

Focus resolution logic in refresh_snapshot:
```rust
let agent_id_opt = match self.focus_lock {
    FocusLockMode::Manual => {
        if snap.agent_count > 0 {
            world.agents().iter_handles().nth(self.focused_agent_cursor % snap.agent_count)
        } else { None }
    }
    FocusLockMode::TopPredator => {
        snap.leaderboard.first().and_then(|e|
            world.agents().iter_handles().find(|h| h.data().as_ffi() == e.label)
        )
    }
    FocusLockMode::Oldest => {
        snap.oldest.first().and_then(|e|
            world.agents().iter_handles().find(|h| h.data().as_ffi() == e.label)
        )
    }
};
```

Benefits of unified focus resolution:
- Single code path for brain activation extraction regardless of mode
- Gracefully handles None case (agent_id_opt) when no agent available
- Mode switching has no special-case logic; simply returns different agent_id
- Works correctly when leaderboards empty (early simulation) or entire population dies

Edge cases handled:
- Focused agent dies while in TopPredator mode: automatically switches to new top predator next frame
- Multiple agents tied for top score: leaderboard.first() provides deterministic selection
- Population extinction: agent_id_opt becomes None, brain display clears gracefully
- Mode switch while paused: immediately updates display without requiring tick advancement
- Arrow keys in TopPredator/Oldest modes: still work via Manual mode switch (press `m` first)

User workflow examples:
1. **Evolutionary strategy research**: Press `t` to lock to top predator, observe brain patterns over 100 ticks as environment changes, note which output channels activate during successful hunts
2. **Survival analysis**: Press `o` to lock to oldest agent, watch brain adapt to declining energy, see sensor patterns shift from exploration to conservation
3. **Comparative analysis**: Press `t` for 10 seconds (observe aggressive patterns), then `o` (observe defensive patterns), then `]` to cycle layers comparing activation differences
4. **Population dynamics**: Start in TopPredator mode, watch brain visualization change as leaderboard turnover occurs (agents rise/fall), correlate with mortality panel events

LAYER NAME DISPLAY: Visual navigation for multi-layer networks

Added "Layers" line in Insights panel showing all layer names with current selection indicator:
- Format: "Layers >nf.layer.0  nf.layer.1  nf.layer.2" (example for NeuroFlow 3-layer network)
- `>` character marks currently selected layer (activation_layer_index)
- Layer names pulled from BrainLayerView.name field (newly added)
- Falls back to "L0", "L1", "L2" indices if name field is None
- Only displays when area.width > 48 (requires medium-wide terminal)
- Positioned after Focus line, before brain activation rows for logical flow

Why layer names matter:
- **Multi-layer networks**: NeuroFlow can have input layer, multiple hidden layers (32, 16, 8), output layer
- **Navigation feedback**: Users pressing `]` to cycle layers need to know which layer they're viewing
- **Architecture understanding**: Layer names encode network topology (nf.layer.0 = first hidden, nf.layer.1 = second hidden)
- **Debugging**: When reporting visualization issues, users can specify "layer nf.layer.2 shows artifacts" vs "layer 2"
- **Comparative analysis**: "mlp.state" vs "nf.layer.0" immediately reveals brain type difference

BrainLayerView now stores layer name:
- Added `name: Option<String>` field to BrainLayerView struct
- from_activations() extracts layer.name from ActivationLayer, wraps in Some()
- vec_from_activations() maps all layers preserving names
- Fallback to None for brains not providing names (backward compatibility)

Layer labels construction:
```rust
let mut layer_labels = String::new();
for (i, layer) in self.snapshot.brain_layers.iter().enumerate() {
    if i == self.activation_layer_index {
        layer_labels.push('>');
    } else {
        layer_labels.push(' ');
    }
    if let Some(name) = &layer.name {
        layer_labels.push_str(&format!("{}  ", name));
    } else {
        layer_labels.push_str(&format!("L{}  ", i));
    }
}
```

Display logic:
- Two-space separator between layer labels for readability
- Current layer gets `>` prefix (visual indicator), others get space (alignment)
- Trimmed at end to remove trailing spaces
- Styled with palette.header_style() for "Layers" prefix, raw text for labels

Width threshold rationale:
- area.width > 48 ensures sufficient horizontal space for typical layer names
- Below 48 columns, layer labels would overlap with other content or wrap awkwardly
- Narrow terminals (80 cols) still show basic brain visualization, just omit layer list
- Wide terminals (120+ cols) have room for full layer names like "nf.layer.0 encoder dense"

HELP PANEL BACKDROP: Improved readability and visual hierarchy

Dimmed full-screen overlay behind help panel:
- Renders Block::default() with dimmed black background over entire frame.area()
- Only rendered when help_visible is true (after pressing `?` or `h`)
- Applied before drawing help panel itself, creating layered effect
- Uses Color::Black with Modifier::DIM for 50% opacity appearance
- Falls back to no background if palette.has_color() is false (monochrome terminals)

Why backdrop matters:
- **Contrast**: Help text was previously rendered directly over busy simulation content (world map, metrics, sparklines)
- **Readability**: White text on black backdrop easier to read than white-on-multicolor
- **Focus**: Dimmed backdrop signals modal state; user attention drawn to help content
- **Professional polish**: Layered overlays feel more modern than raw text-over-content

Implementation details:
```rust
if self.help_visible {
    let size = frame.area();
    let overlay_style = if self.palette.has_color() {
        Style::default().bg(Color::Black).add_modifier(Modifier::DIM)
    } else {
        Style::default()
    };
    frame.render_widget(Block::default().style(overlay_style), size);
    self.draw_help(frame);
}
```

Style selection logic:
- palette.has_color() checks if terminal supports colors (true for xterm-256color, false for monochrome)
- Colored terminals: black background + DIM modifier creates semi-transparent effect
- Monochrome terminals: default style (no background) prevents visual corruption
- Block::default() creates borderless full-screen rect

Layering order:
1. Normal frame content rendered (world, panels, metrics)
2. If help visible: dimmed backdrop painted over everything
3. Help panel drawn on top of backdrop with clear white text
4. User sees: dimmed simulation underneath, crisp help text on top

Alternative approaches considered:
- Clear widget instead of dimmed backdrop: would lose simulation context, feels jarring
- Transparent help box: insufficient contrast, text still hard to read over busy content
- Border-only help: doesn't solve readability issue, border competes with underlying content
- Dimmed backdrop chosen for optimal readability + context preservation balance

HELP DOCUMENTATION UPDATES

Added focus mode key documentation:
- "m/t/o    Focus mode: Manual / TopPredator / Oldest" line added after brain navigation controls
- Consistent formatting: keys/description pattern matching existing entries
- Positioned logically: brain navigation section groups all brain-related controls
- Concise description covers all three modes in single line (space-efficient)

Help panel already documents:
- [ / ]: Cycle brain layers
- ↑ / ↓: Page brain heatmap rows
- ← / →: Change focused agent (manual mode)
- m/t/o: Focus mode switching (NEW)

Complete brain visualization workflow now documented end-to-end in help panel.

RENDER WHITESPACE CLEANUP

Removed extraneous blank lines in scriptbots-render/src/lib.rs:
- No functional changes, pure formatting
- Removed blank lines after function definitions (render_brain_card, paint_brain_radar, format_keystroke, paint_history_chart, append_arc_polyline, paint_header_badge)
- Removed blank line after clear_selection method
- Improves code density and scrolling ergonomics
- Matches Rust style conventions (single blank line between items)

INTEGRATION AND TESTING

Focus mode testing:
- Launch terminal: cargo run -- --mode terminal
- Wait for population to establish (50+ agents, 200+ ticks)
- Press `t`: verify brain display updates to top predator, check focus legend shows agent # changing as leaderboard shifts
- Press `o`: verify brain switches to oldest agent, watch age counter in status
- Press `m`: verify arrow keys work again, manual cursor control restored
- Check event log shows "Focus: Top predators", "Focus: Oldest agents", "Focus: Manual"

Layer name display testing:
- Launch with NeuroFlow: cargo run -- --mode terminal --neuroflow-hidden 32,16
- Expand terminal to 120+ columns
- Verify "Layers" line appears showing ">nf.layer.0  nf.layer.1  nf.layer.2  nf.layer.3"
- Press `]`: verify `>` indicator moves right, brain visualization changes
- Press `[`: verify `>` indicator moves left
- Shrink terminal to 48 columns: verify "Layers" line disappears gracefully

Help backdrop testing:
- Press `?` to open help
- Verify dimmed black overlay covers entire screen
- Verify help text is crisp and readable against backdrop
- Check that simulation content underneath is still faintly visible (not completely obscured)
- Press `?` again to close: verify backdrop disappears, simulation content fully visible
- Test on monochrome terminal: verify no visual corruption from unsupported color codes

Dynamic focus tracking validation:
- Start in TopPredator mode (`t`)
- Watch top predator agent (note its position in world minimap)
- Wait for that agent to die (combat or starvation)
- Verify brain display instantly switches to new top predator (different position)
- Observe brain activation patterns change (different neural state)

MIGRATION AND COMPATIBILITY

No breaking changes:
- FocusLockMode is internal to TerminalApp
- BrainLayerView.name is Option<String>, defaults to None
- Existing code treating BrainLayerView without names still works (falls back to L0, L1...)
- Help panel backdrop only affects visual appearance, no API changes
- Render whitespace changes have zero functional impact

For users upgrading:
- New keys m/t/o available immediately, no config needed
- Default mode is Manual (preserves existing behavior)
- Layer names display automatically if terminal wide enough
- Help panel backdrop automatically improves readability
